question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/alive') does not distinguish between a file written by the legitimate pinger process and one written by an attacker process, allowing the attacker to impersonate the pinger's heartbeat and mask the fact that the real pinger has been replaced with a tampered version?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of atomic file replacement in os.WriteFile('/tmp/alive', ...) to observe a TOCTOU window between the write and the healthcheck's os.Stat() call, injecting a stale timestamp that causes the liveness probe to fail and restart the pinger at a moment chosen by the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not validate that the pinger process is actually running (e.g., via /proc) before reporting liveness, allowing an attacker who has replaced the pinger binary with a malicious one to keep the liveness probe passing by maintaining /tmp/alive freshness?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the world-readable nature of /tmp/alive (permissions 0644) to read the RFC3339Nano timestamp written by the pinger, inferring the exact transaction submission schedule and timing attacks against the Hiero network to front-run or replay submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary exits with code 2 for unknown modes (fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, 'usage: healthcheck [ready|live]')) to cause the orchestrator to interpret this as a probe failure and restart the pinger, interrupting a transaction that is currently in the GetReceipt() waiting phase?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the default mode fallback (mode := 'ready' when len(os.Args) == 1) to invoke the healthcheck without arguments and always get a readiness check, bypassing the liveness check entirely and allowing a stale pinger to remain in service while submitting tampered transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not verify the integrity of the pinger's configuration (e.g., toAccountID, operatorKey) before reporting readiness, causing the readiness probe to pass while the pinger is configured to send transactions to an attacker-controlled account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /tmp/ready is created with os.WriteFile and 0o644 permissions to delete and recreate this file from another process in the same container namespace, causing the readiness probe to cycle between ready and not-ready states and disrupting the transaction submission loop?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not check whether the pinger's ticker is actually firing (i.e., whether submitWithRetry() is being called) before reporting liveness, allowing a deadlocked pinger to appear alive while no transactions are being submitted?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the liveness check uses time.Since(fi.ModTime()) which depends on the system clock to manipulate the system clock (e.g., via NTP spoofing in a misconfigured environment) to make a stale /tmp/alive appear fresh, keeping a tampered pinger alive indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not verify network connectivity to the Hiero node before reporting liveness, allowing a pinger that has lost its gRPC connection (and is therefore failing all Execute(client) calls) to appear alive while all transactions are silently failing?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's stderr output (fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, err)) leaks internal error messages about /tmp/alive to container log aggregators, allowing the attacker to monitor probe failures and time their transaction tampering to avoid detection?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not use any authentication or signing to verify that /tmp/alive was written by the legitimate pinger process, allowing any process with write access to /tmp to forge the heartbeat and mask a compromised transaction submission pipeline?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode only checks /tmp/alive and not /tmp/ready, allowing a scenario where the pinger is live (heartbeat fresh) but not ready (no /tmp/ready), causing the orchestrator to keep a pinger running that is submitting transactions without proper initialization?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not verify the pinger's operator account balance before reporting readiness, causing the readiness probe to pass while the operator account has insufficient funds, leading to all submitted transactions failing with INSUFFICIENT_PAYER_BALANCE?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not check the pinger's retry state before reporting liveness, allowing a pinger that is stuck in the backoff() retry loop (up to 30 seconds per attempt) to appear alive while transactions are being delayed or dropped?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary uses os.Exit() without deferred cleanup to cause resource leaks when the probe fails, and by repeatedly triggering probe failures (e.g., by deleting /tmp/alive), exhaust file descriptors or goroutines in the pinger process, eventually causing transaction submission to fail?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is a separate process from the pinger and communicates only via filesystem state (/tmp/ready, /tmp/alive) to inject false state by writing these files from a compromised init container, causing the pinger to be considered ready before its operator key is loaded?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not verify the pinger's connection to the mirror node REST API before reporting readiness, causing the readiness probe to pass while the pinger is using a stale or tampered network map from buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() that routes transactions to malicious nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's liveness check has a fixed 2-minute staleness threshold to predict exactly when a killed pinger will be restarted, and time a transaction replay attack to occur during the restart window when the pinger is not submitting transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not log the result of its probe to a tamper-evident audit log, allowing an attacker to repeatedly manipulate /tmp/alive and /tmp/ready without leaving any trace of the probe manipulation in the system logs?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not verify the pinger's transaction submission rate before reporting liveness, allowing a pinger that has been throttled (e.g., by a malicious node returning BUSY status) to appear alive while its effective transaction submission rate has been reduced to zero?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not check whether the pinger's context has been canceled before reporting liveness, allowing a pinger that has received SIGTERM and is shutting down (ctx.Done()) to still pass the liveness check if /tmp/alive is fresh, causing the orchestrator to not restart it?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not verify the pinger's last successful transaction receipt before reporting liveness, allowing a pinger that is submitting transactions but receiving only error receipts (e.g., INVALID_ACCOUNT_ID) to appear alive while all transactions are effectively tampered or invalid?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not validate the content of /tmp/alive (only its modification time) to write a file with a future modification time using a crafted syscall, causing the age calculation (time.Since(fi.ModTime())) to return a negative value that passes the > 2*time.Minute check indefinitely?
